chapters.title: What is C++?
chapters.content:
C++ is a powerful, high-performance programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1979 as an extension of C.

C++ gives you direct control over memory, making it one of the fastest languages. It is used for:
  • Game engines (Unreal Engine is C++)
  • Operating systems and drivers
  • Compilers and interpreters
  • Embedded systems
  • High-frequency trading
  • Graphics engines and simulations

C++ is compiled directly to machine code — no virtual machine needed. With great power comes great responsibility: you manage your own memory.

chapters.title: Hello World
Variables & Types
Pointers & References
Classes & OOP
STL — Standard Library
Memory Management
chapters.content:
C++ gives you direct control over memory.

Stack memory (automatic):
  int x = 42;          // auto-freed when scope ends
  { int y = 5; }       // y is gone after }

Heap memory (manual):
  int* p = new int(42);    // allocate
  delete p;                // free — REQUIRED

  int* arr = new int[10];  // allocate array
  delete[] arr;            // free array

Memory leaks happen when you forget to delete.
Use smart pointers to avoid this (modern C++):

  #include <memory>
  unique_ptr<int> p = make_unique<int>(42);
  // auto-deleted when p goes out of scope!

  shared_ptr<Dog> d = make_shared<Dog>("Luna", 3);
  // deleted when last shared_ptr to it is gone

Rules:
  • Every new needs a delete
  • Every new[] needs a delete[]
  • Prefer smart pointers in modern C++
  • Never delete the same pointer twice
